coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots reinforced polypyrrole/ graphene nanoplatelets on flexible eggshell membranes as electrodes of all-solid flexible supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Moreno Araújo Pinheiro Lima, Ravi
de Oliveira, Helinando Pequeno
coredata.dc:description: The improvement in the electrochemical performance of flexible electrodes for supercapacitors depends on adequate doping level of pseudocapacitive prototypes incorporated into truly flexible devices. The interaction of graphene nanoplatelets, carbon dots and polypyrrole on flexible supports of eggshell membranes offers an adequate combination in which redox reactions and fast charge separation contribute to the reversible storage of energy. The mutual incorporation of carbon dots and graphene nanoplatelets in overall structure of PPy-based electrodes returned a capacitive retention of 96.3% (after 2000 cycles of use) and areal capacitance of 173.81 mF cm−2. Superior mechanical resistance and good performance at different bending conditions are additional advantages related to the chemical modification of eggshell membranes as supports for electrodes of supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitors
Polypyrrole
Carbon dots
Graphene
EDLC

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultrasonic incorporation of GNP and carbon dots on eggshell membranes
Preparation of polypyrrole-based composites for flexible electrodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially supported by the Brazilian agencies FINEP ( 04.13.0042.03 ), CAPES (Rede Elinor), FAPESB ( BOL 0585/2018 ), FACEPE ( APQ-0616-1.05/15 ) and CNPq ( 201639/2011-4 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scanning electron micrographs of pristine eggshell membrane- ESM (a), chemically polymerized PPy on ESM (sample ESM + PPy) (b), impregnated graphene nanoplatelets on eggshell membrane (sample ESM + GNP) (c) and chemically polymerized PPy on covering layer of graphene nanoplatelets (sample ESM + GNP + PPy) (d).

Distribution of size of carbon dot nanoparticles (CDs) in water measured by DLS technique (a), stress-strain curves for pristine ESM and chemically modified ESM (sample GNP + CD + PPy) (b), photos of supercapacitors at different bending condition (c) and FTIR spectra of electrodes – samples PPy, PPy + CD, GNP + PPy, GNP + CD + PPy (d).

Cyclic voltammograms for supercapacitors based on electrodes of (a) PPy, (b) GNP + PPy, (c) PPy + CD and (d) GNP + CD + PPy at different scan rates: 5 mV s−1 (black), 10 mV s−1 (magenta), 25 mV s−1 (wine), 50 mV s−1 (red), 75 mV s−1 (yellow), 100 mV s−1 (green), 200 mV s−1 (violet) and 300 mV s−1 (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cyclic voltammograms measured at 50 mV s−1 for supercapacitors based on electrodes of (a) PPy, (b) GNP + PPy, (c) CD + PPy and (d) GNP + CD + PPy. Shaded regions characterize the relative contribution of capacitive term on overall response.

GCD curves at different current density (lines in red – 1 mA cm−2; magenta – 2 mA cm−2; green – 3 mA cm−2; black – 4 mA cm−2 and orange 5 mA cm−2) for supercapacitors prepared using electrodes of PPy (a), GNP + PPy (b), PPy + CD (c), GNP + CD + PPy (d). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Nyquist plots for supercapacitors prepared with electrodes of PPy (black squares), PPy + CD (red circles), GNP + PPy (green up triangles) and GNP + CD + PPy (blue down triangles), (b) Ragone plot for supercapacitors prepared with electrodes of PPy (black squares), PPy + CD (red circles), GNP + PPy (green up triangles) and GNP + CD + PPy (blue down triangles), (c) Specific capacitance as a function of current density for supercapacitors for supercapacitors prepared with electrodes of PPy (black squares), PPy + CD (red circles), GNP + PPy (green up triangles) and GNP + CD + PPy (blue down triangles), (d) capacitance retention for sample GNP + CD + PPy at repeated charge/ discharge cycles at 10 mA cm−2 – in the inset it is compared the response of samples in the Nyquist plot before (black) and after (red) 2000 consecutive uses, (e) CV curves at scan rate of 100 mV s−1for sample GNP + CD + PPy under different bending states and (f) variation of specific capacitance for sample GNP + CD + PPy under different bending states. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of areal capacitance for GNP-CD-PPy device with corresponding systems reported inthe literature.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots reinforced polypyrrole/ graphene nanoplatelets on flexible eggshell membranes as electrodes of all-solid flexible supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Science, Federal University of Sao Francisco Valley, 48920-310, Juazeiro, BA, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Federal University of Sao Francisco Valley

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Materials Science, Federal University of Sao Francisco Valley, 48920-310, Juazeiro, BA, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The improvement in the electrochemical performance of flexible electrodes for supercapacitors depends on adequate doping level of pseudocapacitive prototypes incorporated into truly flexible devices. The interaction of graphene nanoplatelets, carbon dots and polypyrrole on flexible supports of eggshell membranes offers an adequate combination in which redox reactions and fast charge separation contribute to the reversible storage of energy. The mutual incorporation of carbon dots and graphene nanoplatelets in overall structure of PPy-based electrodes returned a capacitive retention of 96.3% (after 2000 cycles of use) and areal capacitance of 173.81 mF cm−2. Superior mechanical resistance and good performance at different bending conditions are additional advantages related to the chemical modification of eggshell membranes as supports for electrodes of supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have become hot research topics in recent years in energy storage devices due to their typical advantages on conventional capacitors and batteries, such as the ability to storage energy in short interval of time (higher specific power than batteries). As a frontier of knowledge, it is pursued environmentally friendly behavior, long cycle life, light weight and flexible devices with superior specific power and specific energy [1,2].

The energy storage mechanisms in supercapacitors are classified into two categories: pseudocapacitance and electrical double layer capacitance (EDLC) effects [3,4]. The electric charge in EDLC is stored in the double layer formed at the electrolyte/ electrode interface – this process is favored by electrostatic interaction between ions and charged surface of the electrode [5]. As a consequence, the EDLC-based systems present high reversibility degree for charge accumulation – consequence of high surface area that favors the non-Faradaic reaction.

On the other hand, the pseudocapacitance makes use of redox reactions on surface/ near-surface of the electrode (Faradaic charge transfer), being the conducting polymers - CPs (such as polypyrrole and polyaniline) potential candidates for pseudocapacitors. In particular, polypyrrole presents a unique π-conjugated structure [6], characterized as an environmental stable system, with good conductivity and ease of manipulation [7]. Despite these advantages, there are shortcomings to be overcome in CP - based systems, such as low cyclability and poor mechanical properties. The typical strategy to improve these limitations in polymer-based supercapacitors is based on the synthesis of hybrid composites with nanoparticles of metal oxides and carbon derivatives. The association of EDLC and pseudocapacitance requires a synergistic interaction of components to improve not only the surface area but also the effective charge transfer at electrolyte/electrode interface.

In particular, scarce attention has been dedicated to the interaction of conducting polymers and carbon dots (CD) in electrodes of supercapacitors. Carbon dots are zero dimensional structures with carbon core and functional groups at the edges in quasi-spherical format nanoparticles (diameter less than 10 nm) that have been progressively reported as fluorescent markers [8–16]. CDs present ultrafast electron transfer to conducting polymers and graphene oxide, a property that can be explored in the improvement of the electron transport at electrodes/electrolyte interface [17,18], introducing high surface area and good conductivity level for effective electron transfer between electrode and electrolyte [19].

Different strategies have been explored for the synthesis of PPy/CD complexes: Jian et al. [7] reported the electrostatic self-assembly of CDs on oppositely charged polypyrrole, while Xie et al. [6] reported the electrochemical growth of polypyrrole on carbon dots. In both cases, the charge transfer resistance decreases and the morphology is affected by improved surface area for charge transference and accumulation.

In addition, the development of flexible and wearable devices depends on adequate combination of truly flexible supports with high available surface area for charge accumulation in low-cost prototypes. Based on these requirements, the circular economy recognizes the use of waste-based materials as an important step to ``close the loop''. Eggshell is an abundant material dispersed as waste and characterized as a rich structure of fibers with a diversity of sites that favor the chemical functionalization.

On the other hand, the low conductivity of eggshell membranes (ESM) can be circumvented by incorporation of carbonaceous materials such as graphene nanoplatelets that impregnate the fibers improving its mechanical and electrical properties. For this, the ultrasonication favors that shockwaves accelerate carbon dots and graphene derivatives in direction to the porous fibers of ESM, increasing the available surface area and conductivity level of supports for posterior step of chemical polymerization.

Recent work developed by our group revealed that poor mechanical properties of polypyrrole can be circumvented by adequate deposition of polymeric layers on flexible eggshell membrane [20], that offers a rich distribution of functional groups in a porous fibrillar structure for polymeric growth.

The adequate combination of EDLC from graphene and carbon dots and the pseudocapacitance of polypyrrole reinforce the electrochemical properties of electrodes applied in the production of high performance supercapacitors. Similar procedure has been explored in the production of wearable supercapacitors on textiles [21,22].

Herein, it is explored the influence of carbon dots and graphene nanoplatelets on overall response of polypyrrole-based supercapacitors deposited on ESM supports. For this, it is established the ultrasonic-assisted incorporation of CDs and graphene nanoplatelets previously to the chemical polymerization of polypyrrole, for modification of porous degree in ESM templates with mutual interaction of CDs and graphene derivatives and polymeric covering layer.

To evaluate the contribution of CDs and graphene nanoplatelets on overall electrochemical performance of supercapacitor, cyclic voltammetry curves at different scan rate were deconvoluted [23]. Dunn et al. [24] derived the kinetics from the general case i(V) = aνb – current at fixed potential as a linear combination of the most common coefficients (b = 1 and b = 1/2), as follows:
(1)
i
( V )
=
k 1
ν +
k 2
ν
1 / 2
where V is the potential, ν the sweep rate (in
mV
s
− 1
) and k 1 and k 2 are parameters that distinguish capacitive and diffusive-active parameters, respectively. Based on this method, it is possible to estimate for each voltage the relative contribution of capacitive and current-diffusive on overall response, allowing that relative area in CV curve can be distinguished from ν dependent (capacitive behavior) and ν 1/2 dependent (diffusive-active current term).

The chemically modified electrodes were characterized from SEM images, BET assays, DLS experiments for determination of carbon dot size, FTIR, mechanical assays, cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge-discharge experiments, Nyquist diagram and capacitive retention after successive charge-discharge procedures and bending efforts.

Polyvinyl alcohol, ferric chloride, pyrrole, camphorsulfonic acid, graphene nanoplatelets grade C (GNP – surface area 750 m2 g−1) were all of analytical grade (acquired from Aldrich, USA) were used as received except for the pyrrole which was distilled before the use. Hydrochloric acid (Quimica Moderna, Brazil), ethanol, dichloromethane and acetone (Dinâmica, Brazil) were used as received. The ultrapure water used in all of experiments was obtained from an ultrapurification system (Millipore, USA) with resistance of 18 MΩ.

The structure of materials was scrutinized from Fourier transform infrared spectrum (KBr method) acquired in an IR Prestige-21 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer Shimadzu. Mechanical assays (force – deformation curves) were performed in a Universal Machine of Assays Digital Line EMIC. The distribution of size of the carbon dots was evaluated by dynamic light scattering in a Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 Malvern. Changes in the available surface area due to the additives incorporation were determined by BET method, with experiments conducted in Gemini 2390A Micromeritics (USA) equipment.

The electrochemical characterization of the devices was performed in a two-electrode configuration system with samples disposed between parallel plates of a Solartron 12,962 sample holder connected to a potentiostat Autolab PGSTAT 302 N (Metrohm), from which were evaluated the cyclic voltammetry curves (scan rate in the 5 mV s−1 – 300 mV s−1 range and 0–0.8 V of potential window), galvanometric curves (current in the 1–5 mA range) and impedance spectrum (in the 1 Hz – 1 MHz frequency range). Ultrasonication was performed in a LimpSonic equipment (Brazil) operating at 40 kHz/ 35 W.

The (areal or specific) capacitance was evaluated according Eq. (2) [25]
(2)
C x
=
2
D a
I
V 2
X
where V is the potential below the IR drop (for discharge), X assumes two values:
X = A
– the area of the electrodes (applied in the calculus of areal capacitance (CA ) or
X = m
– the active mass of electrodes for specific capacitance evaluation (Cm )), I is the applied current for discharge and Da is the area under the discharge curve.

The specific energy and specific power were calculated as follows [25]:
(3)
E D
=
C m
V 2
2 * 3600
(4)
P D
=
3600
E D
Δ
t
where ED is the specific energy (in
W
h
g
− 1
), PD is the specific power
(
W
g
− 1
)
, Cm is the specific capacitance and Δt is the discharge time. Capacitance retention assays were performed considering GCD curves continuously imposed on supercapacitors at charge/ discharge current of 10 mA/−10 mA, while electrochemical performance under different bending states was evaluated from CV curves at
100
mV
s
− 1
.

The removal of calcium derivatives from eggshell and the step of ESM extraction were obtained according Ref. [20] in which is established an acidic treatment, as follows: the eggshell residues donated by a local market (Juazeiro, Brazil) were freshly washed with water to eliminate contaminants. After that, the resulting material was immersed in an aqueous solution of HCl (1 M) under stirring for 30 min. The resulting membranes are washed with ultrapure water for removal of additional residues. The electrodes are separated in a standard size of 1 cm × 1 cm and stored in milli-Q water for posterior use.

Carbon dots were synthesized according procedure reported by Sahu et al. [26], in which the nanoparticles were produced from hydrothermal treatment of orange juice in ethanol. Following this procedure, 40 mL of orange juice was mixed with 30 mL of ethanol. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 120 °C for 150 min. After that, the solution was separated to reach the ambient temperature. The resulting material was washed with dichloromethane (for removal of the unreacted components) and then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min. Acetone was incorporated in the supernatant and the resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. Resulting solution of carbon dots was stored under dark condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasonic incorporation of GNP and carbon dots on eggshell membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The graphene ink was prepared from dispersion of 100 mg of GNP in 100 mL of ethanol which was kept under ultrasonication for 60 min.

The incorporation of carbon derivatives (graphene nanoplatelets and carbon dots) into the ESM fibers was explored using the ultrasonic procedure, as follows: the ESM was immersed into 5 mL of GNP ink which was kept under sonication. After each interval of 1 h of sonication, the samples were removed from solution, dried and washed freshly with water to remove unbounded nanostructures. This process was repeated four times to provide a more complete process of covering by graphene nanoplatelets.

The incorporation of CDs on ESM supports was established as follows: a solution containing 5 mL of as-prepared carbon dots received ESM supports which were submitted to the same procedure of impregnation as previously reported for GNP. Corresponding procedure was explored for mutual incorporation of GNP and CD on ESM. In this case, the ink was prepared with 2.5 mL of initial solution of carbon dots and 2.5 mL of mother solution of GNP (1 mg mL−1 of GNP). After the additives impregnation step, the samples ESM + GNP, ESM + CD and ESM + GNP + CD were prepared and stored under dark for posterior polymerization. The value of mass loading of graphene in ESM electrodes (1 cm × 1 cm) obtained was in order of 500 μg while the amount of incorporated CD was in order of 700 μg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polypyrrole-based composites for flexible electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The polymeric growth of polypyrrole on ESM substrates was provided by the interfacial polymerization of PPy on previously modified ESM supports. For this, 0.251 g of camphorsulfonic acid was dispersed in water and then sonicated for 30 s. After that, 175 mg of FeCl3 were dispersed into the solution and kept under intense magnetic stirring. The second phase was composed by 3 mL of hexane and 50 μL of pyrrole. After this step, both solutions were kept at 3 ~ 5 °C. Then, the organic phase was slowly verted on aqueous solution – the polymerization takes place at 4 °C for 4 h. After that, the resulting electrodes were rinsed with milli-Q water to remove unreacted species. The value of mass loading of polypyrrole on previously treated electrodes (1 cm × 1 cm) is in order of 3.8 mg.

The solid electrolyte applied as separator was prepared as follows: polyvinyl alcohol (1.0 g) was immersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water and kept at 90 °C for 2 h to reach adequate dispersion in water. After that, the solution returned to the ambient temperature and received 1 mL of phosphoric acid (85 wt%). Then, an aliquot of this solution – 4.0 mL (with careful removal of air bubbles) was transferred to a silicone mold and allowed to stand for 48 h, favoring the complete evaporation of solvent. After this process, a solid and transparent film was removed from mold and applied as a separator of two identical electrodes of 1 cm × 1 cm.

SEM images of electrodes were performed at different steps of preparation to evaluate the influence of components on overall morphology of composites. For comparison, in Fig. 1 a is shown the image of pristine support (eggshell membrane) that is composed by a distribution of fibers with diameter of few micrometers disposed in a net with abundant distribution of pores. Scarce grains disposed on fibers (clearly identified from images) are CaCO3 residues not removed from acidic treatment.

If considered the direct polymerization of polypyrrole on eggshell membrane, as shown for sample in Fig. 1b, it is observed the formation of a covering layer on natural fibers, followed by the growth of aggregates (in forms of grains superposed on ESM fiber - see Fig. 1b) for sample ESM + PPy. The aggregation represents a drawback to be circumvented due to the overpolymerization of polypyrrole and following reduction in the surface area for charge accumulation.

The impregnation of graphene nanoplatelets on ESM introduces important advantages on chemical properties of assembled device. The SEM of ESM surface impregnated with GNP is shown in Fig. 1c. As can be seen, the ultrasonic incorporation of GNP on ESM support affects the morphology of natural fibers with changes in the texture of surface (presence of small aggregates).

This process results in a strong increase in the surface area of support. As measured by BET technique, the surface area for pristine ESM is in order of 2.41 m2 g−1. The following step of ultrasonic impregnation with GNP increases the surface area to values in order of 15.03 m2 g−1. The strong increase in surface area of composite represents an important advantage in terms of new active sites for charge accumulation.

In addition to the increase in surface area, these changes on morphology of ESM affect the kinetics of polymerization. SEM images for samples that received a polymeric layer on previously deposited GNP (samples designed as ESM + GNP + PPy) – see Fig. 1d reveal a more regular covering degree of superposed layers (GNP + PPy) in which is observed the absence of aggregates of PPy that mutually covers the GNP layer and natural fibers, providing a material with lower level of aggregation of polymeric phase.

As expected, it was not possible to identify the presence of carbon dots in the structure of electrodes – since it is predicted to be observed as nanoparticles with dimension in order of few nanometers. To characterize the nature of carbon dots incorporated in composites, we performed dynamic light scattering assays to measure the typical diameter of nanoparticles. The histogram shown in Fig. 2 a indicates that produced carbon structures are in nanoscale dimension, characterized by a single population of nanoparticles with radius centered in 2.8 nm. This result confirms that synthesized particles are typically 0D structures with high surface area that favors the overall structure for charge accumulation.

In addition to these important results related to nanosize dimension of carbon derivatives, it is worthy to mention that mechanical properties of support are also favored by additives incorporation. As one can note in Fig. 2b, the stress-strain diagram for pristine and chemically modified ESM confirms that reinforcement in the mechanical properties is provided by chemical polymerization and additives incorporation on ESM. Moreover, electrodes with high degree of flexibility are produced with chemical modification, as illustrated in Fig. 2c.

FTIR spectroscopy was explored to scrutinize the characteristic signature of materials. As show in Fig. 2d, the polymerization of polypyrrole can be considered from the presence of peaks at 1541 cm−1 – assigned to CC stretching vibration in the PPy backbone, peaks at 1452 cm−1 and 1165 cm−1 related to CN stretching vibration, peak at 1037 cm−1 assigned to CH in-plane vibration band and 970 cm−1 related to CC in-plane bending of pyrrole ring [20,27–33].

As previously mentioned, electrochemical capacitors make use of two distinct mechanisms to storage energy: the conventional electronic/ ionic charge separation at interface electrode/ electrolyte – characterizing the response of EDLC systems and reversible faradic reaction on electro-active species (surface functional groups on conducting polymers). The interaction of EDLC and pseudocapacitance effects on high surface area supports has been considered as important approaches applied in the optimization of overall response of supercapacitors, conveniently characterized by cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge-discharge curves, Nyquist plots and from derivative parameters such as specific energy and power, discussed as follows.
The cyclic voltammetry is a powerful electrochemical tool for identification of prevailing electron transfer process as a function of time and applied potential.

Faster scan rate of potential decreases in size the diffusion layer on electrodes originating higher current from two distinct processes: the current established from diffusing elements (variation with ν1/2) or adsorbed species (variation with ν). In agreement with the aforementioned, one can see that faster scan rate returns increasing current for all of samples (as shown in Fig. 3 ) – higher value of current is observed at higher scan rate (at the same potential). On the other hand, the area enclosed by these curves is proportional to the capacity (in Coulomb). The shape of voltammogram at different scan rate is strongly affected by prevailing Faradaic/ non-Faradaic processes. As one can note in Fig. 3, more prolate curves prevail at higher scan rate and for electrodes prepared in the absence of carbon dots and graphene nanoplatelets revealing the pseudocapacitance contribution of PPy-based samples. It is worth mentioning that current reaches a maximum value for samples prepared in the presence of carbon dots. By comparison of the current reached at the same scan rate, it is possible to identify the following order: GNP + CD + PPy > GNP + PPy > PPy + CD > PPy.

This order indicates that combined action of CD and GNP introduces adequate surface modification of ESM for chemical polymerization of polypyrrole, producing high performance electrodes for supercapacitors.

As previously described, an important tool to quantify the kinetics performance of devices was developed by Dunn et al. [24] with the deconvolution of two processes - the diffusion-controlled and non-diffusion limited terms from CV curves. Using this model, it is possible to calculate the contribution of exponent term b = 1 on overall response – characterizing the relative contribution of non-diffusion-controlled process or ``surface'' process that is reinforced by incorporation of nanomaterials in electrodes. Curves shown in Fig. 4 a–d are CV diagrams for different devices at scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The shaded region characterizes the relative contribution of non-diffusive controlled process (b = 1 – capacitive contribution). As one can see, strong contribution for capacitive behavior is introduced by carbon dots that elevate the capacitive contribution in overall response to values above 80% - measured from relative area of enclosed curve. It suggests that incorporation of nanoparticles and nanoplatelets favor the charge accumulation on its surface (nanosize effect) associated with minimal aggregation level of covering layer of PPy on these structures.

However, it is worthy to mention an important aspect: as reported by Augustyn et al. [34], care should be taken regarding the use of this mathematical method for separation of diffusion and capacitive terms in nanoscale-based systems. Despite the dominant “ν” contribution observed in Fig. 4c and d, the double layer contribution (bulky capacitance measured from impedance spectrum) is not affected by carbon dots incorporation and the deviation from a vertical line in Nyquist plots for all of samples revealed that pseudocapacitance still dominates after chemical modification with graphene nanoplatelets and carbon dots. It means that incorporation of carbon derivatives offers available area for adsorption of electrolyte ions but also optimizes the electrical transport for pseudocapacitive processes.

Based on these findings, it is possible to observe that high current reached in Fig. 3 for samples GNP + CD + PPy at fixed scan rate is a direct consequence of strong electrochemical contribution from GNP + CD layer. The influence of these properties on parameters such as specific capacitance and specific energy/power can be calculated from galvanostatic charge/discharge assays.

All of samples were characterized at different current density (in the range of 1 mA cm−2 to 5 mA cm−2). Corresponding curves are shown in Fig. 5 a–d – as one can observe, the charge-discharge follows a straight line (unique slope) – clearly identified for curves at higher current density and a negligible IR drop – as result of low internal resistance. Another critical parameter to be considered from these results refers to the longer discharge time (higher electrochemical performance for energy storage) clearly identified for samples prepared in the presence of CD, GNP and CD + GNP, revealing that processes of interaction between components were important to improve the electrochemical performance of resulting devices (if the total charge stored in device is proportional to the area in CV curves, the specific and areal capacitance are proportional to the slope in GCD curves).

Both diagrams (CV and GCD curves) are strongly dependent on internal resistance of samples – measured from Nyquist plots, (shown in Fig. 6 a). These curves reveal relevant information concerning to the intrinsic properties of devices – such as bulk resistance – measured in the diagrams as the intersection of the curves with the real part of impedance (Z´ axis – Fig. 6a). As one can note, the bulk resistance of the samples follows the order: RPPy > RPPy+CD > RGNP+PPy > RGNP+CD+PPy with values distributed in the 2 Ω to 3.5 Ω range, characterizing electrodes with low internal resistance, as observed from negligible IR drop measured in the GCD curves. It is worth mentioning that incorporation of carbon derivatives (GNP and CD) affects positively the charge transport mechanisms allowing that the lowest value for internal resistance can be observed for sample GNP + CD + PPy.

These results are sufficient to calculate the specific energy and power of devices. The plot of specific energy vs. specific power at different current density (the Ragone plot) can be explored as an important diagram to optimize both parameters in the direction to a device with superior storage of energy and power in comparison with conventional batteries and capacitors.

Based on calculus of specific energy and specific power (see Eqs. (2) and (3)) the construction of the Ragone plot returns the following order for the best performance in terms of power and energy: GNP + CD + PPy > GNP + PPy > PPy + CD > PPy (see Fig. 6b) that is in agreement with order of specific capacitance as a function of current density (see Fig. 6c). As one can see, the best response is observed for supercapacitors of GNP + CD + PPy that reach a capacitance of 70 F g−1 presenting a slight decrease with current density.

The combination of high capacitance and good performance in terms of specific energy /specific power is a result of adequate interaction of effective reactions at pseudocapacitance components associated to fast charge separation in CD and GNP- based samples. All of these results confirm the critical role introduced by carbon dots and graphene nanoplatelets on overall electrochemical performance of supercapacitors – if considered superior performance in terms of internal resistance, specific capacitance and specific energy/ power as a result of synergistic interaction of capacitive-like and pseudocapacitive-like additives.

The response of material under continuous use represents another important parameter to be considered in the project of device. The cyclability of supercapacitor was verified under current density of 10 mA cm−2. As previously observed for PPy-based devices (see Ref. [25]) and shown in Fig. 6d, the capacitance proves an increase at initial cycling procedure (500 cycles) in response of the self-activation of the device (new pathways for ion circulation) - this step is followed by typical degradation of the device. For GNP + CD + PPy supercapacitor, it is observed a slight degradation (capacitance retention of 96.3% of initial value) that is a consequence of increase in the internal resistance of electrodes (as shown in inset of Fig. 6d, the impedance of the device increases after 2000 cycles of use in response of degradation of polymeric layers to successive cycling of device under high current density).

To evaluate the influence of flexibility of supercapacitor on its overall electrochemical performance, the flexibility of supercapacitors was tested in CV assays performed with device at different bending stage. As can be seen from Fig. 6e, a slight reduction is observed in the area of CV curves revealing that under progressive bending less charge is stored – provoking reduction in the performance of supercapacitors, as expected. The corresponding variation in the specific capacitance is summarized in Fig. 6f. As shown, at high bending state (θ = 135º) the capacitive retention is maintained in 69% from initial value, characterizing this system as a promising prototype of flexible device.

For comparison with state-of-art in devices based on carbon dots and conducting polymers, Table 1 summarizes the areal capacitance of the best supercapacitor, prepared in this work with corresponding systems (CD-based and polymer-based supercapacitors). By comparison with reported values, it is possible to identify that at high current density (1 mA cm−2) the system GNP-CD-PPy returned a competitive value for areal capacitance (173.81 mF cm−2), characterizing the composite prepared with mutual incorporation of GNP and CD as the most promising candidate for flexible supercapacitors applications, operating at high current density regime.

An important aspect to be considered from this comparison is that low cost devices prepared on supports of eggshell membrane reach outstanding performance at high current density, introducing the flexibility as a potential advantage for a new generation of devices in which the concept of circular economy is completely considered (since the choice of substrate (ESM) until the nanoparticles (CDs derived from orange juice)).

The incorporation of carbon derivatives on flexible substrates previously to the chemical polymerization of polypyrrole represents an important strategy to produce flexible electrodes with improved mechanical and electrochemical properties. Superior areal capacitance of 173.81 mF cm−2 is obtained for prototypes as a consequence of increase in the non-diffuse terms, critically assigned to high surface area offered by carbon dots and graphene nanoplatelets. Moreover, the synergistic interaction of polypyrrole, carbon dots and graphene nanoplatelets favors the good performance in capacitive retention (96.3%) for devices operated at high current density (in order of 10 mA cm−2) with high performance at different bending states

Ravi Moreno Araújo Pinheiro Lima: Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Helinando Pequeno de Oliveira: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the Brazilian agencies FINEP (04.13.0042.03), CAPES (Rede Elinor), FAPESB (BOL 0585/2018), FACEPE (APQ-0616-1.05/15) and CNPq (201639/2011-4).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.est.2020.101284.
